### Traditional communications and the cliff effect

Early wireless communication systems leveraged analog signals to transfer information, meaning that the information was modulated onto a continuous (with respect to both time and signal amplitude) waveform. Analog communication systems still see wide use today, with a prominent example being FM radio.

However, in the 1990s digital wireless communication systems began to take shape. Rather than continuously modulating the carrier waveform with the information to transmit, the continuous signal is first "broken up" in time (sampled) with each sample's amplitude then mapped to discrete levels (quantized). These discrete chunks constitute a digital signal which can then be transmitted using a finite set of pre-selected waveforms. Instead of the reciever needing to estimate a continuum of values from a noisy signal (hard), it now only needs to decide which waveform from the pre-selected set was sent (easier--with proper waveform design).

This benefit, among others, led to digital becoming the standard protocol for nearly all of our modern wireless communications systems. However, analog systems do have one interesting advantage over digital counterparts. Analog systems show graceful degredation under worsening channel conditions--think of an FM radio station slowly fading into static as you move further from the broadcast station. The signal may get worse as you move away, but for a time you will still be able to make out the audio on the channel. Digital systems have a more "all or nothing" property about them--consider a satellite TV signal that abruptly cuts off during a heavy rainstorm. Under a certain threshold, the signal abruptly becomes entirely unusable. This is known as the "cliff effect" and is visualized below:

::: {#cell-fig-cliff .cell execution_count=1}

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Communication performance as a function of channel quality. Analog systems degrade gracefully, while digital systems fall off a 'cliff' once channel quality drops below a threshold.](index_files/figure-html/fig-cliff-output-1.png){#fig-cliff width=566 height=374}
:::
:::


### Digital communications and separate source-channel coding

At a high level, digital communication systems are typically structured in the following way:

[TODO-FIGURE]

- The *source* is the raw digital signal which has undergone sampling and quantization
- The role of the *source coder* is to compress raw information into a more efficient representation
- The role of the *channel coder* is to make the compressed signal more robust to the inevitable degradation from the wireless channel
- The corresponding modules at the receiver perform the inverse operations to hopefully recover the original source signal

One question we might ask is, "Why have seperate functions for source and channel coding and not just combine them into a single module?" This would lead to a conceptually simpler system with potentially improved performance resulting from the joint design and optimization. The answer consists of two parts. First, Claude Shannon showed in one of the most important results of Information Theory, the separation theorem, that a separate source-channel coding scheme is actually optimal under certain theoretical conditions. While these conditions are never the case in practice, a separated scheme has historically still generally worked pretty well. Second, source and channel coding perform much different functions, with both having well-developed methods resulting from decades of research. Jointly optimizing the two analytically is generally an intractable problem.

Still, the demands of today's wireless use cases and the continual push for better systems has led some to revisit this question.
